questions.question: What is the 'relapse-prevention mindset' for anger management?

options.text: Believing you will never get angry again after completing the course
Accepting that setbacks will happen and focusing on catching anger sooner, de-escalating faster, and repairing more effectively
Avoiding all situations that could possibly trigger anger
Starting the course over from the beginning every time you get angry

questions.explanation: The relapse-prevention mindset means expecting setbacks while recognizing that the skill isn't perfection — it's catching yourself sooner than you used to, de-escalating faster, repairing more effectively, and learning from each episode. All-or-nothing thinking ('I lost it again — I'm hopeless') leads to giving up.

questions.question: What is the most important element of a genuine apology after an anger episode?

options.text: Explaining all the reasons why the other person provoked you
Saying 'I'm sorry if you were offended' to acknowledge their feelings
Taking specific responsibility for your behavior without excuses or 'but' statements
Promising that you will never get angry again

questions.explanation: A genuine apology involves taking specific responsibility ('I'm sorry I raised my voice. That wasn't okay'), acknowledging the impact, and owning it without excuses. Avoid 'but' — 'I'm sorry I yelled, but you pushed my buttons' erases the apology. Instead: 'My reaction is my responsibility, regardless of what triggered it.' Then commit to a specific change.

questions.question: How is trust rebuilt after a pattern of harmful anger?

options.text: Through one heartfelt conversation that resolves everything
Through consistent changed behavior over time, not through words alone
By demanding that others recognize your progress immediately
By avoiding the person until they forget what happened

questions.explanation: Trust is rebuilt through consistent behavior over time, not through words alone. If anger has been a long-standing pattern, others may be cautious about trusting that change is real — and that's understandable. Keep showing up differently even when it's not immediately acknowledged, and don't use your efforts as leverage.

questions.question: Which of the following is part of maintaining long-term anger management progress?

options.text: Only practicing techniques when anger is above an 8
Daily practice of slow breathing, using the HALT check, and weekly self-check-ins about anger patterns
Completely eliminating all sources of stress from your life
Avoiding all people and situations that have ever made you angry

questions.explanation: Maintaining progress involves daily practice (a few minutes of slow breathing to keep baseline arousal low), situational preparation (using the HALT check before stressful situations), post-episode review (learning from each episode), weekly check-ins (noticing patterns), and lifestyle foundations (sleep, exercise, meals, and monitoring substance use).

questions.question: When anger leads to violence, threats, or coercive control, what kind of help is needed?

options.text: General anger management education is sufficient
No help is needed — this is a normal part of relationships
Specialized intervention (such as batterer intervention programs) rather than just anger management
The victim needs to change their behavior to prevent triggering the anger

questions.explanation: Anger that leads to violence, threats, coercive control, or ongoing intimidation is abuse and requires specialized intervention, such as batterer intervention programs — not just standard anger management. The person's partner or family members may need safety planning regardless of good intentions to change. This goes beyond anger management into territory that requires guided professional intervention.

title: Long-Term Anger Management and Repairing Relationships - Quiz
